data.title: Apple: Better Now, But Don't Buy
data.description:
Article Thesis

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL) has been extremely volatile in recent weeks, and while shares soared on Wednesday, they trade well below the highs from the end of 2024. While Apple's valuation has come down compared to the highs seen last year, AAPL is still far from a bargain today, and it remains highly exposed to macro threats such as tariffs or an escalating Taiwan conflict.

Past Coverage

I have written about Apple in the past here on Seeking Alpha, most recently in January, around one quarter ago. I gave the company a "Sell" rating back then, which has worked out fine so far, as AAPL is down by around 20% since that article was published. I was bearish mainly due to Apple's very elevated valuation at the time, while macro concerns such as tariffs were not really on the horizon yet. With Apple's valuation coming down and with the macro picture, especially related to trade tensions with China, changing, it is time for an update.

Apple: An Extremely Volatile Giant

Apple is one of the largest companies in the world in terms of market capitalization, and yet it almost trades like a small-cap in the very recent past—its volatility is very elevated. While many investors see Apple's shares as very low-risk and stable, they have been among the weakest performers in the Mag 7 group of stocks this year:

In the above chart, we see that Apple delivered the second-weakest year-to-date return among the Mag 7 stocks, with only Tesla (TSLA) faring worse. Prior to Wednesday's massive market rally, Apple was down by more than 30%, but even following that partial recovery, AAPL is still down by more than 20%.

Apple Is Exposed To Macro Headwinds

In recent days, tariffs have gotten a lot of attention, and rightfully so. After all, the tariffs that were instated by the new US President, Donald Trump, could impact a wide range of companies, as well as consumers, substantially. As of Wednesday afternoon, many tariffs have been postponed for 90 days, but the 10% base tariffs are still in play. Also, importantly, tariffs on China are still in place and have even been increased to a now very high level of 125%, and China's tariffs on the US are in place as well.

Apple, of course, is a US-based company, but that doesn't mean that it is immune to these tariffs. iPhones, as well as some of the other consumer hardware that Apple sells, are manufactured in several countries—one of them is China, so tariffs on China do have an impact on Apple. Even beyond the direct financial impact of tariffs, there could be headwinds: Tariffs could disrupt global trade flow, for example, which could result in supply chain issues for a giant like Apple with its very large and diverse supply chain.

As a company that is seen as very "American", Apple could also be a prime target of reciprocal actions. If the Chinese government were to retaliate against rising US tariffs, they could move against US tech companies, for example, e.g. by putting restrictions on who can buy an iPhone, or what apps can be downloaded on the App Store, and so on.

Europe, with which the US has some trade tensions as well, is another major economy that could move against big US tech companies, including Apple—there has been some talk recently about the EU potentially putting additional restrictions or taxes on US tech companies. The EU could, for example, try to take a larger share of Apple's App Store revenue in Europe via some form of a digital tax.

As a reaction to the US tariffs on many other countries (although some of them have now been postponed), consumers in these countries may also be less willing to buy one of Apple's products. Chinese consumers, for example, could be angered by the very large tariffs the US has imposed against China, which could make Chinese consumers opt for non-Apple products when they buy their next smartphone, notebook, and so on. Some of this sentiment exists in Europe and other markets as well; thus, this could be a headwind outside of China, too—although I would not be surprised if China is the country with the most pronounced impact.

Another risk factor, although a not very likely one, I believe, is an escalating Taiwan conflict. Apple does not only depend on China as an end market for its products and as a manufacturer of its hardware, but Apple is also extremely dependent on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) for chip production. If the Taiwan conflict were to escalate—this is, I believe, unlikely but not impossible—then Apple's supply chains would be disrupted in a massive way.

While there are several macro risks and headwinds for Apple, the company also has some advantages or mitigating factors. Its brand is very strong, and its products are generally in the premium segment, which gives Apple considerable pricing power. Tariff-related cost increases can thus, at least partially, be passed on to consumers, I assume. Apple also has high margins, so if tariff tensions were to result in some margin headwinds, profits would not fall off a cliff. If a company with a net margin of 2% sees its margins drop by 200 base points, all profits would vanish, for example, but Apple with its net margin of around 24% over the last year would be less impacted by some margin deterioration. Also, Apple has a clean balance sheet with a considerable cash position, which helps reduce risks further—in case we get into a recession, Apple has the financial resources to stomach such a situation.

Apple's Valuation: Better, But Still Not Good

When Apple's shares peaked at around $260 at the end of 2024, its valuation was very high—based on current estimates, the company will earn around $7.20 to $7.30 this year; thus, Apple was trading at 36x net earnings a couple of months ago. For a large and established company that is not growing very fast any longer, that wasn't justified at all, I believe.

Following the recent sell-off, Apple is now trading at a little less than $200 per share, which pencils out to an earnings multiple of around 27, which means an earnings yield of 3.6%. An earnings yield like that is not especially attractive compared to treasury yields of more than 4%, but at least the valuation is now not as excessive as it was a couple of months ago.

Looking at Apple's enterprise value to EBITDA ratio, which also accounts for cash and debt on the balance sheet, we see the following:

The multiple has come down to the high teens range and is now around 10% higher than it was, on average, over the last decade. This suggests that right now is still not an especially good time to buy—shares are still somewhat on the expensive side. But right now, shares are trading way closer to the historically normal or fair valuation range than they did a couple of months ago, when the enterprise value to EBITDA multiple was way above 20.

Of course, both the EBITDA estimate for this year and the earnings per share estimate for this year could decline to some degree going forward due to the expected tariff impact, but no matter what, Apple below $200 is not as overvalued as it was way above $200 per share.

Takeaway

Apple has not been a safe haven asset in the recent past, even though many investors see it that way. Shares have been very volatile, and AAPL has underperformed the Mag 7 average so far this year.

The company could be impacted by tariffs and trade tensions in several ways, but at least its valuation has declined, and it looks less overvalued now compared to before the sell-off. I still don't deem it an attractive investment right here, but am less bearish than I was earlier this year.

data.title: The Tariff Pause
data.description:
By Warren Patterson | Ewa Manthey

Energy – Tariff pause provides some relief

Oil prices rallied along with risk assets yesterday after President Trump paused reciprocal tariffs on most trading partners, at least those that haven’t retaliated yet. This puts tariffs back to the baseline 10%. However, there was no relief for China. The US increased tariffs to 125% after China raised retaliatory tariffs on US goods to 84%. While the pause offers some relief to markets, there’s still plenty of uncertainty on the trade front. This uncertainty is still likely to drag on global growth, which is clearly a concern for oil demand. Still, conditions are not looking as bad as they were just a few days ago.

The ICE Brent forward curve is signalling a better-supplied oil market, at least across parts of the forward curve. While the front end of the curve is in backwardation, it has shifted into contango from the January 2026 contract onwards. Meanwhile, the Brent Dec-25 - Dec-26 spread has fallen into contango. All signs point to a softer market balance a little bit further down the road.

US inventory data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) shows that crude oil inventories increased by 2.55m barrels over the last week. This takes stocks to a little over 442m barrels -- the highest since July. Meanwhile, crude stocks at Cushing grew by 681k barrels, leaving stocks at the WTI delivery hub at their highest level since November. The slightly bearish crude numbers were offset by refined products, with gasoline and distillate stocks falling by 1.6m barrels and 3.5m barrels, respectively.

European natural gas prices came under significant pressure yesterday, with TTF falling by more than 7%. However, the tariff pause should see the gas market opening higher today. Meanwhile, the latest positioning data shows that investment funds have been heavily selling TTF. Funds sold 19.7TWh over the last reporting week to leave them with a net long of 126.3TWh -- the smallest net long since May last year.

Metals – Copper rallies on tariff pause

Copper and other base metals rallied in early morning trading today following President Trump’s 90-day tariff pause. Clearly, though, there’s still plenty of uncertainty as tariffs against key metal consumer, China, have been raised to 125%. A prolonged trade war would drag on consumer confidence, weaken appetite for risk and weigh on demand for raw materials. However, the prospect of a prolonged trade war has also raised expectations for Beijing to unveil more aggressive stimulus measures. This could cap the downside to copper and other industrial metals.

Agriculture – WASDE expectations

The USDA is scheduled to release its monthly World Agricultural Supply and Demand Estimates (WASDE) report later today. The market expects the agency to increase its US soybean ending stocks estimate by 2m bushels to 382m bushels, while trimming its corn ending stocks estimate by 28m bushels to 1,512m bushels. Global ending stock estimates are expected to see little change. For corn, stocks are expected to decline from 288.9mt to 288.3mt, while soybean and wheat ending stock estimates are expected to rise to 121.9mt (vs. 121.4mt) and 260.4mt (vs. 260.1mt), respectively.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: AptarGroup: Still Bearish... For Now
data.description:
For years now, I have been a fan of the packaging industry. This is an interesting business that should grow as the broader economy grows. Profits and cash flows do have a tendency to be volatile. But many of these companies historically trade at multiples that I would consider to be attractive. Of course, not every player in this space has been appealing. In early December of last year, I decided to downgrade AptarGroup (NYSE:ATR), a niche packaging solutions provider for the pharmaceutical industry, the beauty space, the food market, and the personal care industry. Prior to that, I had the company rated a ‘hold’. However, I believed that by late last year, shares had gotten too expensive.

This led me to downgrade it to a ‘sell’. This was in spite of the fact that the business was continuing to grow at a nice pace. Since then, the stock has fallen by 18.7%, which is significantly more than the 16.0% downside achieved by the broader market. This move lower comes in light of expectations from management that the company could be faced with some near term weakness. Even though the stock does not appear expensive on an absolute basis, it is quite pricey compared to other similar firms. Add on top of this the aforementioned uncertainty about the future, and I do think that maintaining it as a soft ‘sell’ candidate makes sense right now.

Even though I am taking a bearish stance at this moment, it is important to note that my mindset on the matter could change as new data comes in. It just so happens that, before the market opens on May 2nd, management will be reporting financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts expect a decline in sales and profits. In the event that the company performs better than expected, and if guidance indicates that weakness moving forward might not be warranted, I would argue that an upgrade to a ‘hold’ rating would be appropriate.

Not time for an upgrade yet

The most recent fundamental data that we have regarding AptarGroup covers the final quarter of the company's 2024 fiscal year. During this time, revenue for the company hit $848.1 million. That represents an increase of 1.1% compared to the $838.5 million reported one year earlier. This rise in sales can really be attributed to two of the company's three operating segments. The disappointing one was its Aptar Beauty segment, which saw revenue drop from $287.7 million to $274.1 million. Management blamed this on an unfavorable change in product mix and lower tooling sales. Prestige fragrance and skin care technologies revenue also declined.

The other two operating segments performed quite nicely. The largest of these, which is the Aptar Pharma segment, saw revenue climb from $385.1 million to $400.7 million. Management said that this was because of increased demand for proprietary drug delivery systems that are used for emergency medicines, central nervous system to therapies, allergic rhinitis, and also due to higher royalty revenue. The picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that a weaker 2023 to 2024 cold and flu season reduced demand for nasal decongestants and saline rinses.

Meanwhile, the company’s Aptar Closures segment saw revenue climb from $165.7 million to $173.3 million. While the sales increase there was roughly 5%, actual core sales jumped by 7%. This was because of foreign currency fluctuations that negatively impacted revenue on a year-over-year basis. According to management, the rise in core sales was thanks to a variety of end markets, with management specifically identifying the food and beverage space as a key driver.

With revenue rising, profitability improved also. Net income jumped from $62.4 million to $100.9 million. Even on an adjusted basis, we got a nice increase from $80.9 million to $103.2 million. Other profitability metrics also improved year over year. Operating cash flow, for instance, did decline from $219.6 million to $178.2 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $137.2 million to $173.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $179.4 million to $194.9 million.

When it comes to 2024 as a whole, the business reported growth across the board. With the Aptar Pharma segment seeing revenue climb from $1.52 billion to $1.64 billion, and with the Aptar Closures segment reporting a rise from $698.8 million to $714 million, the business saw overall revenue pop from $3.49 billion to $3.58 billion. Frankly, the picture would have been better had it not been for the fact that the Aptar Beauty segment reported a decline in revenue from $1.27 billion to $1.23 billion.

On the bottom line, AptarGroup saw growth as well. Net income jumped from $284.5 million to $374.5 million. The rise in adjusted profits was from $320.6 million to $381.6 million. We saw operating cash flow grow from $575.2 million to $643.4 million, while the adjusted figure for that grew from $569.5 million to $675.7 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $707.7 million to $774.9 million. In the chart above, you can see how the company's profit margins have been improving as of late. And in the chart below, you can see how the matter has evolved on a segment-by-segment basis.

The fact of the matter is that management has been investing heavily into restructuring some of its operations. In 2023, it allocated $45 million into these activities. It invested another $13 million in 2024. Most of this money has been dedicated to the Aptar Beauty segment as the company contends with a difficult demand environment. In fact, over the last two years, the firm allocated $28.7 million this segment. However, it has not ignored its other segments. Most notably, it has put $21.8 million toward the Aptar Closures segment. Clearly, these investments are paying off.

In the absence of any meaningful guidance for the year as a whole, I believe that the best way to value the company is to use historical results for the last two completed fiscal years. In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced using three different valuation approaches. Usually, these are multiples that I would consider to be in the fair value range, though the price to earnings multiples do indicate that the company might be overpriced. In the table below, I stacked it up against five similar firms. And what I found, sadly, was that it ended up being more expensive than any of them.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
AptarGroup 25.8 14.6 13.7
Berry Global Group (BERY) 17.4 7.3 8.4
Silgan Holdings (SLGN) 19.9 7.6 11.1
Crown Holdings (CCK) 25.4 9.1 10.0
Greif (GEF) 15.8 9.5 8.3
Ardagh Metal Packaging SA (AMBP) 10.7 4.1 8.2

This certainly has been the driving force behind my pessimism for the company. However, there is also some uncertainty moving forward. Management mentioned that 2025 should be a strong year for the company, even saying that double-digit earnings growth is on the table. However, they mentioned that early on in the year the business could be negatively impacted by foreign currency fluctuations and higher effective tax rates, the latter of which should involve its operations in France. Softer demand in some end markets should also prove problematic. They didn't go so far as to provide any guidance when it came to revenue. However, they did say that adjusted earnings per share for the first quarter of the year would likely be between $1.11 and $1.19.

To put this in perspective, for the first quarter of 2024, AptarGroup reported adjusted earnings per share of $1.26. Analysts expect weakness as well, with a reading of $1.16 per share. This would translate to a decline from $85.2 million to $78.8 million. In the table above, you can also see other financial metrics for the first quarter of 2024. Analysts believe that revenue will be around $894.3 million. That would represent a drop from the $915.4 million reported the previous year. Earnings per share can also be expected to fall from $1.23 to $1.15, which would mean a drop in profits from $83.1 million to $78.1 million. In all likelihood, the cash flow metrics will drop as well. But we will have to wait until management reports these results before the market opens on May 2nd.

The good news is that management is not letting this expected weakness to stop them from buying back shares. In October of last year, the company announced a new $500 million share buyback program. During the final quarter of the year, they repurchased 218,000 shares in exchange for $37.3 million. Given how the stock is currently priced, I would much prefer that the company allocate this capital toward growth initiatives or buying up cheaper competitors. But the fact that management is allocating such a significant amount of cash toward buybacks indicates that they are at least not materially pessimistic about the future.

Takeaway

At the end of the day, I do think the picture has improved for AptarGroup. However, there is a lot of uncertainty regarding the business at this time. Broader economic concerns certainly need to be monitored, and the stock is expensive compared to other similar enterprises. Because of these factors, I think that maintaining the company as a soft ‘sell’ candidate makes sense. But at some point, upgrading it to a ‘hold’ could be in the cards.

data.title: AOD: High Yield, Trading At Steep Discount, Buy For Gains
data.description:
As the stock market crash slashed valuations to the lowest level since the last bear market, it is the time to take advantage of investors fear by investing in beaten down stocks, ETFs and funds with the strong growth potential over long term. abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (NYSE:AOD) is one of the best investment vehicles with a potential to offer significant returns. In addition to its 15% dividend yield and monthly dividends, the fund’s shares are trading at a steep discount with the potential to rebound over the long term. Therefore, I am initiating coverage of AOD with a buy rating.

Stock Market Crash Presents a Buying Opportunity

Although the current stock market crash wiped out trillions of dollars in the past month, the downside has cooled valuations. Before the selloff, the S&P 500 was trading around 22.5x forward earnings, the highest level since late 2021. The S&P 500 is currently trading around 18x forward earrings. In the last bear market, the S&P 500’s forward PE declined to around 16x.

I believe there is more downside left as the US economy is likely to experience a recession in 2025. In the case of recession, the market is expected to fall further and trade in a bear territory for a short time. However, history suggests that bear markets are short in nature and market rebounds to the new record levels over the long term.

On the positive side, the Fed is likely to slash interest rate at a faster pace than expectations, which, I believe, will play a significant role in fuelling recovery. Jan Hatzius, Goldman Sachs chief economist, also expects the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates three times to 3.5-3.75% by the end of 2025. However, in the case of recession, he anticipates 130 basis points of rate cuts this year. CME data also signals 100 basis points of rate cuts in 2025.

How Does AOD Presents an Attractive Buying Opportunity?

As the uncertainty is likely to last in 2025, it is a great strategy to consider beaten down investment vehicles with a high dividend yield. Dividends will enable investors hold the security while waiting for a price rebound over the long term. In addition, the dividend-paying companies generally have a higher potential to cope with market cycles.

abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund, a closed-ended equity mutual fund, appears to be a top investment option for a buy and hold strategy. AOD trades at a significant discount compared to its net asset value. Its shares are currently trading around $7.40 per share, down from its net asset value of $9.56, representing a discount of more than 22%. In addition, the fund’s price is down nearly 27% from its recent peak of $10.14.

The decline in its price is attributed to the fund's exposure to the technology, finance, consumer cyclical and communication sectors. These sectors has been hit harder in the current selloff. On the positive side, these high beta sectors also have the potential to outshine the broader stock market index during recovery and bull run.

AOD uses fundamental analysis with a combination of bottom-up and top-down approach to create its stock portfolio. The fund’s top holding includes prominent names, such as Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO), JPMorgan (JPM), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Goldman Sachs (GS), Tencent Holding (OTCPK:TCEHY), TJX Companies (TJX), and others. Moreover, dividend-paying companies like Lowe’s (LOW), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Linde Plc (LIN), AstraZeneca (AZN), Bank of America (BAC), Roche Holding (OTCQX:RHHBY) and Cisco Systems (CSCO) are next in the list of its top holdings.

All these companies have well-established business models with the potential to generate steady growth over the long-term. The most of these companies are leaders in their respective markets. In addition, its portfolio is also concentrated in consumer defensive, healthcare, industrials and utilities sectors. The fund’s stake in these sectors help it lower the portfolio volatility in the downtrend.

Furthermore, I also don't see a risk to its dividend stability. This is because of its stake in fundamentally sound and well-established companies. These companies have the potential to sustain their dividend growth despite short-term volatility, For example, Microsoft and Apple are sitting on $71 billion and $166 billion in cash and cash equivalent, while Broadcom and Alphabet had $9.31 billion and $95.66 as of the end of fiscal 2024.

In addition to the hefty cash balances, their free cash flows offer a significant gap for more dividend increases. For instance, in 2024, Broadcom and Alphabet generated $19 billion and $72.76 billion in free cash flows. The majority of other companies also appear in a position to increase their dividends. For example, JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, TJX Companies have a payout ratio based on incomes of 25%, 28% and 35%, respectively. Whereas, companies from the consumer defensive, healthcare, industrials, and utilities sectors are less correlated to the market cycles. Therefore, the risk of the dividend cuts is limited.

Risk Factors to Consider

Like other asset classes, AOD also carries a risk factor. Its portfolio exposure to high beta stocks can increase the downside movement in the tech-driven selloff. Furthermore, the fund is also exposed to market cycles. It has a beta of slightly less than 1%. It is also exposed to tail events and other external risk factors.

Final Thoughts

The stock market corrections and crashes occurred frequently in history. The market even recovered from the most scary crashes like the Great Depression and Financial crisis. Investors who can tolerate short-term volatility and deploy money in beaten-down assets with strong fundamentals, often generate massive gains over the long term. I believe it is a great time to invest in stocks, ETFs and funds. Their prices are significantly down from their recent peak and valuations appear cheap. AOD is one of the best investment vehicles due to its high-yield and diversified portfolio of fundamentally sound stocks. Moreover, its shares are trading at a significant discount compared to its net asset value.

data.title: Chubb Faces Manageable But Real Risk From Trade Wars (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Chubb (NYSE:CB) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 8%, though they have not been immune to the tariff-related sell-off, dropping about 10% from the highs. As an insurer, I view its business with manageable impact from tariffs, but I see its China exposure as an increasing risk. Since I last covered shares in November, CB has lost 3%, which is still significantly better than the market’s 17% drawdown. Given new macro developments, now is a good time to revisit CB.

In general, the insurance sector should be largely immune from the direct impact of tariffs. As a rule of thumb, tariffs focused on the cross-border trade of goods, rather than services. Additionally, insurers generally are not selling products across borders, as their operations are subjected to local regulation and done through a local legal entity. As you can see below, Chubb is one of the most global US insurance companies, getting over 40% of its business from overseas. Still, I do not see cross-border tariffs having any material impact on its ability to write new business given these dynamics.

That said, trade tensions do pose indirect risk. First, the global trade war has thus far been focused on tariffs, but in some areas, there is a non-zero risk of non-tariff escalation. The most likely source of this is in the US-China relationship. Because China is planning retaliatory tariffs, President Trump suggested Monday he could raise tariffs further on China. Indeed, he did just that, raising tariffs to 104%. Following this, China increased its tariff rate to 84%. On Wednesday, while easing tariffs on other countries, Trump raised tariffs on China again.

We are now at a 125% tariff on Chinese goods, while China is at 84%. While China can match tariff rate hikes, given the nature of the imbalance, there is no way for China to respond in an economically proportional way through tariffs, in my view. The US imports about 3x as much as it exports from China, so symmetrical tariff rates do not really equalize the situation. Instead, President Xi may need to seek unconventional responses, which is what sparked speculation of China selling treasury bonds or banning Hollywood films. Beyond this, he could simply limit or stop the export of rare earth minerals or increase regulatory scrutiny of American companies that have a presence in China. The worse the US-China relationship gets, the more one must consider the possibility of more significant non-tariff actions.

Chubb is one of the few insurers with a meaningful China presence. It operates in China via is ~80% ownership in Huatai Life Insurance and Asset Management. Huatai Asset Management has about $125 billion in assets under management (AUM). In Q1 of 2024, it increased its ownership stake by 9% for $555 million, giving its stake a roughly $5 billion value. That translates to about $13 a share.

I see a modest risk at this time of the Chinese government confiscating stakes or forcing stake sales by American companies, escalation would likely have to spiral several times before that occurs. However, China can certainly direct state-owned-enterprises and citizens to reduce business with American companies, impairing the value of stakes like Chubb’s. At this time, out of caution, I am haircutting the value of Chubb’s China operation by 25-50%, which is $3-6 a share, or about a 1-2% reduction in the company’s overall fair value.

Tariffs also indirectly impact insurance companies insofar as they cause inflation. Insurers lose money based on the frequency of claims as well as the severity. Tariffs should not impact the frequency of claims, but if they make cars and construction materials more expensive, they can increase their severity. In the longer-term, insurers can reprice premiums higher to recapture this lost margin. However, because policies are written for 6-12 month periods typically, there can be a lag, which causes underwriting income to be reduced.

Indeed, you can see this quite clearly after COVID, when an unexpected surge in inflation was not met by higher insurance premiums for another few months. During 2021 and 2022, insurance profits were squeezed as profits rose faster than inflation, and then insurers recovered margins in 2023 and 2024 as premiums grew faster than inflation. If tariffs raise goods inflation by ~2%, this will likely pressure insurance margins in late 2025 and through mid-2026.

This comes at a time when Chubb has been performing very well. In the company’s fourth quarter, it wrote $12.1 billion of net policies, up 7%. Underwriting income reached a record $1.6 billion as its combined ratio was 85.7% or 82.2% excluding catastrophes. For the year, it earned $22.51 in core operating income, achieving a core return on equity of 13.9%.

As you can see below, Chubb has consistently been a best-in-class underwriter of risk, as its reputation of excellent customer service and willingness to pay claims has actually enabled it to charge higher premiums. Alongside American Express (AXP), Chubb is arguably the only remaining “luxury” brand in an otherwise commoditized financial services industry.

That said, even by its own standards, underwriting results have been extremely strong, aided by premium increases over the past two years. As such, I have already been anticipating some mean reversion in results towards the ~87% area. Given some likely incremental tariff inflation, I feel more confident in underwriting losses rising a bit and do see a risk of a rise closer to 88% than 87% if tariffs are fully implemented for a sustained period of time.

Of course, CB will also face some pressure in Q1 from the wildfires in California, where it had higher exposure given its leading franchise towards higher net worth household. Chubb has estimated it faces $1.5 billion of California losses, for a roughly $3 earnings headwind. Insurers face some catastrophe losses every year, and so if we have a quieter hurricane season, for instance, CB can make back some of these losses, though that remains unknown until Q3/Q4. For now, I assume a normal cat year outside of CA with these losses on top of the normal results.

One other channel that Chubb can be impacted by tariffs is on its $151 billion investment portfolio. Long-term bond yields have actually stayed elevated despite the stock market decline, as there may be more inflation. With its five-year duration, I do not see a material impact to investment yields. CB has seen its portfolio yield rise thanks to higher reinvestment rates, and last quarter net investment income rose by 14% to $1.69 billion, also a record.

If tariffs cause a downturn, that could increase defaults and credit costs. Chubb maintains a fairly conservative investment portfolio with 60% A-rate or higher, which are very unlikely to face losses. There could be some additional losses in its 16% in high yield, but even a 5% increase in losses would be a sub-$1 billion impact realized over several years. Ultimately, there may be some modest headwind, but not a sufficient amount to change the investment view on CB.

Shares do offer a 1.3% dividend yield, which I continue to view as secure, and its share count declined about 1% last year thanks to buybacks. I expect a similar pace of repurchases this year, with CB continuing to retain earnings to support business growth rather than more aggressively buying back stock. Given its history of strong underwriting returns, I am comfortable with its approach to capital allocation.

Last year, I had been targeting at least $24 in 2025 EPS. I now see results of $19.50-$21.25, given ~$3 of California losses and some further underwriting compression from higher inflation. That leaves shares at 13.3x this year’s earnings. I have argued for a 13x multiple, which suggests CB is about fair value. That said, some of its CA losses should prove to be one-time in nature, adding back $2 of these losses to reflect a worse-than-normal cat environment would suggest a fair value of $294.

From this fair value, I am haircutting ~$5 to reflect some risk of impairment in its Chinese business for a resultant fair value of $289. That points to about 5.5% upside, or a ~6.5-7% total return including its dividend. Frankly, this is not enough upside to maintain a “buy” rating, where I target a ~10+% return. As such, I am moving shares to hold. CB’s business is not immune to tariffs, but the impact is manageable. As such, there is no need to sell shares, but after a period of significant relative outperformance, CB is no longer a buy.

data.title: Voya Global Perspectives Market Models - Mutual Fund Series Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Globally diversified core strategy based on the Global Perspectives unique investment philosophy and built with Voya’s suite of proprietary and multi-manager mutual funds.

Key takeaways

  • In the fourth quarter of 2024, U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The U.S. bond market struggled due to concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and a more conservative rate-cut path from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed), which saw the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rise by more than 80 basis points (BP) to 4.58% by the end of the quarter.
  • Global markets face challenges in 2025, but the U.S. remains attractive due to post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains and energy independence, with the Fed expected to continue gradual easing.
  • During 4Q24, the Voya Global Perspectives Market Models: MF Series delivered negative absolute and mixed relative returns.
  • On a net-of-fees basis for the following: The Voya GPMM Income Model outperformed the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Bond Index. The Voya GPMM Conservative Growth MF, Voya GPMM Moderate Growth MF and Voya GPMM Aggressive Growth MF models underperformed the S&P Target Risk Conservative, Moderate and Aggressive Indices, respectively.

Market review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald Trump’s presidential victory. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation, increased deficit spending and the U.S. Fed’s more conservative rate-cut path, as the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 bp, ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

The U.S. election, marked by Trump’s proposed tariffs, increased global uncertainty, lead to further U.S. dollar strength and broadly weighed on international equities. Europe struggled with political instability in France and Germany and rising energy costs ahead of winter. In yen terms, Japanese equities rose due to strong corporate activity including strategic mergers, share buybacks and improved return on equity. Meanwhile, China dealt with weak consumer activity, property sector challenges and concerns about regulatory changes and economic restructuring. These combined factors caused international equities to continue to underperform.

Portfolio review

The Voya GPMM MF Series posted negative absolute and mixed relative returns across models. The Income portfolio performed best from an absolute and relative standpoint.

The models were in base positioning during the period. Asset allocation was a detractor. An underweight in U.S. large cap and overweights to emerging markets (EM), real estate investment trust (REIT) and long government bonds were the main detractors. U.S. stocks delivered another period of positive performance, helped by continued strong corporate earnings. Sharply rising long-term interest rates hurt REIT and bonds. They also contributed to a stronger U.S. dollar, which weighted on EM stocks.

Manager selection was mixed. Strategies that contributed most to performance were Voya High Yield Bond, Voya Large-Cap Growth and Voya Mid Cap Research Enhanced Index. The underlying managers that detracted most were Voya Multi-Manager Emerging Markets Equity, Voya Global Bond and Principal Global Real Estate.

For the quarter ending September 30, 2024—actual company reporting finishing in December 2024—the year over year earnings growth for the S&P 500 companies was 9.1%. Overall, other fundamental market indicators do not suggest overriding the earnings signal is warranted. Therefore, the GP strategy will maintain base positioning and rebalance back to targets in early January 2025.

Outlook

Global challenges and opportunities confront the markets in 2025. However, the U.S. remains attractive driven by post-election optimism, investment, productivity gains from deregulation, innovation and energy independence. Despite some softening, the U.S. labor market remains resilient. Inflation is expected to stay manageable, allowing the Fed to continue its gradual easing.

Supported by this positive outlook and President Trump’s pro-business proposals, we continue to overweight U.S. large and mid-cap stocks. Large-cap valuations appear expensive, but they are supported by strong corporate profitability. The increasing weight of the technology sector with its capital-light business models suggest that higher margins may be the new norm, making expectations more reasonable. With the election concluded, corporate spending is expected to resume and drive positive earnings growth as well. Mid-caps offer enticing valuations and are poised to benefit from a broadening expansion and lower borrowing costs and provide more financial stability than small caps.

Outside the U.S., pockets of weakness are becoming more apparent, particularly in the eurozone and China. The eurozone faces cooling services and ongoing manufacturing challenges, heightening economic uncertainty. In China, new policies aim to address economic sluggishness, especially in the property market, and boost investor confidence. However, the investment environment looks more promising in Japan, where deflation has been curbed and corporate reforms have made progress. Despite this, there has been relatively little foreign investment, creating an opportunity as more investors recognize the growth story.

In fixed income, U.S. investment-grade core bonds offer decent yields and portfolio stability. While conviction on rates near-term direction is limited, we believe core bonds will hedge against equity risk due to the transition from inflation to growth risk that has occurred. While credit stress and defaults are expected to remain controlled, high yield spreads are very tight, leading us to prefer equities for risk allocation. In addition, an expectation that the U.S. dollar rally will continue and a positive U.S. economic outlook justify a preference for U.S. assets and an underweight in foreign currency denominated bonds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: AVSU: Solid Value-Leaning ETF For ESG Conscious Investors
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Avantis Responsible U.S. Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:AVSU) is designed for ESG-focused investors seeking active broad market exposure to all size segments for a reasonable price. Fund managers aim to enhance returns by emphasizing the value and quality factors, but my analysis suggests otherwise, and I couldn't find many instances where AVSU was fundamentally superior to its Russell 3000 Index benchmark or other ESG-themed funds offered by iShares. As a result, I don't think AVSU will gain much traction, so I've limited my rating on it to simple "hold." I look forward to explaining why in more detail below.

AVSU Overview

Strategy Discussion

AVSU launched on March 15, 2022, and has a 0.15% expense ratio and $300 million in assets under management. Compared to its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV), it's a pretty small fund. Other competing ETFs like the iShares ESG Aware MSCI USA ETF (ESGU), the iShares ESG MSCI USA Leaders ETF (SUSL), and the iShares MSCI USA ESG Select ETF (SUSA) had multi-year head starts on AVSU and, not surprisingly, have much larger asset bases. Of these, ESGU is easily the most successful, with $11.49 billion in AUM.

Avantis lists four reasons why you should consider AVSU on its website:

1. Gain exposure to U.S. companies across all size segments that meet specific environmental, social, and governance measures.

2. Enhance expected returns by emphasizing securities trading at lower valuations with higher profitability ratios.

3. Access an efficient portfolio management and trading process that reduces risk and transaction costs.

4. Can "fit seamlessly into an investor's asset allocation."

Avantis follows an exclusions process when selecting securities, which simply means that rather than include stocks that score well on ESG metrics, it excludes stocks that don't score well on ESG metrics. AVSU's prospectus provides some examples, such as:

  • companies with significant revenue (e.g., 5%+) derived from oil and gas production or distribution, civilian firearms, nuclear weapons, palm oil production, tobacco or cannabis, gambling, and adult entertainment
  • companies with any revenue derived from thermal coal
  • companies with no female directors
  • companies with low corporate governance scores

These are just some examples, and Avantis retains the right to exercise discretion when excluding a company. The prospectus also notes how managers primarily use a company's adjusted book-to-price ratio to define value and the cash from operations to book value ratio to define profitability. Again, managers may also rely on other value and profitability ratios, and as an actively managed fund, they can exercise discretion.

AVSU Weighting Scheme

Regarding AVSU's weighting scheme, the prospectus states the following:

To determine the weight of a security within the portfolio, the portfolio managers use the market capitalization of the security relative to that of other eligible securities as a baseline, then overweight or underweight the security based on the characteristics described above.

This language is standard for Avantis ETFs, but Avantis exaggerates how much active weighting actually is happening with AVSU. A quick calculation matching up its components' weights and market caps shows a 95.5% correlation, showing how market caps are still the primary factor behind each component's weights. For reference, here are the correlations for the other four ETFs:

  • IWV: 99.6%
  • ESGU: 97.5%
  • SUSL: 99.7%
  • SUSA: 91.4%

AVSU Composition Differences vs. Peer Group

These high correlation figures don't mean AVSU is cap-weighted like IWV. They only illustrate a positive link between size and weight, and there are still some important individual stock weight differences to consider. Using IWV's top 25 holdings as a baseline, the following table highlights these differences.

As shown, Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) is AVSU's first key exclusion, followed by Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Philip Morris (PM). Notably, AVSU includes Walmart (WMT), which the other three ESG funds exclude, so it doesn't seem like the screening process is too strict. For more aggressive ESG exposure, SUSA is the most effective, as it excludes mega-cap stocks like Amazon (AMZN) and Meta Platforms (META). However, that approach drastically changes the risk/return profile, and that's not Avantis' purpose with AVSU.

ESG funds often take more risk by overweighting Technology companies, as they typically have a low carbon footprint. However, AVSU is different. As shown below, it underweights Technology by 7.18% on average, with the primary offset in Financials, consistent with Avantis' typical "value" lean.

There are also high allocations to Consumer Discretionary stocks like The TJX Companies (TJX) and General Motors (GM), and AVSU is the only fund to have virtually no exposure to Energy. Even SUSA includes some Oil & Gas Storage & Transportation stocks like ONEOK (OKE) and Cheniere Energy (LNG).

AVSU Analysis

Performance Snapshot

Since AVSU launched on March 15, 2022, it has delivered a 13.77% total return, which was about 3-6% worse than its peers. SUSL did the best with a 20.41% gain, but that's only because of its high allocation to Nvidia (NVDA). Most will agree that its historic run-up over the last few years is unlikely to repeat, and with a 28% decline YTD, it's been one of the hardest-hit stocks since President Trump announced his sweeping global tariffs.

Since AVSU has the least exposure to Nvidia, I wouldn't place too much emphasis on this performance chart. However, I also wouldn't assume it's "safer" just because of its valuation lean. Since April 2022, AVSU's volatility, as measured by standard deviation, was the highest of the five at 19.55%, and its 19.92% maximum drawdown was only marginally better and obviously not enough to offset its underperformance in the bull market years.

Fundamental Analysis By Top 25 Holdings

The next table lists selected fundamental metrics for AVSU's top 25 holdings, which comprise 36.47% of the portfolio. Not surprisingly, this concentration level is quite low compared to its peers, highlighting Avantis' preference for designing well-diversified portfolios.

Here are two additional observations to consider:

AVSU: Solid Valuation Discount, Reasonable Growth Rates

The first thing that stands out is AVSU's attractive value ratios. For example, its 15.44x forward P/E represents a 16% relative discount to IWV and even more compared to the three ESG funds. It's a similar story for AVSU's 11.44x trailing P/CF and its 3.06x trailing P/B ratio, so there's little doubt it's a unique option for value-leaning ESG investors. You will have to sacrifice some growth potential - AVSU's historical and estimated sales and earnings growth rates are a few percentage points below, but a lot of those differences are concentrated in mega-cap tech stocks, so for me, it's a fair tradeoff.

AVSU: Below Average Quality

For the second time in as many days, I've found Avantis' quality metrics to be average at best and borderline disappointing. In the table above, I noted AVSU's weighted average ROE, ROA, and net margin figures, which were all below the peer group average. In my view, quality is the #1 factor long-term investors should focus on, and many times, value ETFs sacrifice it too much. To further demonstrate, I've also listed additional quality statistics below, and since AVSU is below average on all of them, I suggest that's the reason for its high volatility and why it hasn't performed better in drawdowns.

In addition to lower margins, AVSU's holdings are slightly more leveraged than its peer group, as evidenced by its 1.22x and 5.62x debt-equity and debt-free cash flow ratios. These figures all feed into AVSU's 8.64/10 sector-adjusted profit, which I calculated using Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, leading to the conclusion that AVSU has sacrificed some quality and growth in pursuit of value. That approach might work, but it hasn't since AVSU's inception and is not my preferred strategy.

Investment Recommendation

AVSU is a well-diversified core ESG fund with a 0.15% expense ratio and $300 million in assets under management. My analysis of its composition indicates the screening process isn't too strict, though it is one of the few ESG funds that completely avoids the Energy sector. On the other hand, some inclusions, like Walmart, are surprising, so perhaps it's not the ideal fund for "responsible" investors, despite its name.

AVSU's track record since its launch three years ago has been underwhelming because of low allocations to mega-cap growth stocks like Nvidia, but looking forward, it can turn things around if markets begin to favor value stocks. Its 15.44x forward P/E ratio represents a solid 16% discount over the Russell 3000 Index, and its lower growth rates probably might not matter much if the economy softens. Still, I was disappointed to find AVSU's holdings had below-average profit margins and above-average debt ratios, which offset the value and diversification benefits the fund offers. As a result, I don't think there's anything too special about AVSU, but it is a decent value-oriented option for ESG-conscious investors, so I've decided to rate it a neutral "hold." Thank you for reading.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/10/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Voya MidCap Opportunities Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Strategy overview

Actively managed mid-cap growth strategy that relies on fundamental research and analysis to identify companies with strong and accelerating business momentum, increasing market acceptance and attractive valuations.

Key takeaways

• In the fourth quarter of 2024, the equity markets experienced mixed returns. Domestic large- and small-cap stocks delivered positive returns, while value indices and international markets saw declines. Cyclical sectors, such as consumer discretionary, financials and technology, outperformed defensive sectors. Growth factors led performance, while value factors declined.

• For the quarter, the Fund underperformed its benchmark, Russell Midcap Growth Index (the Index), on a net asset value (NAV) basis due to unfavorable stock selection.

• The outlook for equities in 2025 is cautiously optimistic as the U.S. economy remains strong and the Trump administration is expected to implement favorable policies in terms of deregulation and taxes. However, risks such as tariffs, inflation and geopolitics may result in volatility.

Portfolio review

U.S. stocks continued their upward trajectory during the fourth quarter following Donald with the S&P 500 Index rising by 2.41% and the Nasdaq Composite advancing by 6.17%. The consumer discretionary and communication services sectors led, while materials and healthcare lagged. Large cap stocks outperformed small caps, and growth stocks significantly beat value stocks.

The U.S. bond market struggled during the quarter on concerns about sticky inflation and cut path. The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index declined by 3.06% and the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose by more than 80 basis points (bp), ending the quarter at 4.58%. The Fed cut rates by 25 bp in November and December. However, the central bank now projects just two rate cuts in 2025, reflecting a more cautious stance in response to strong economic data.

For the quarter, the Fund underperformed the Index on NAV basis largely due to selection effects. The stock selection effects within the industrials, health care and consumer discretionary sectors were the greatest detractors. Alternatively stock selection within the information technology, communication services and materials sectors contributed to performance.

Key contributors to the quarter's performance were AppLovin Corp. (APP), Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) and Nu Holdings Ltd (NU).

An overweight position in AppLovin Corp. (APP) contributed to performance. The stock surged in November following a strong earnings report, with revenue significantly surpassing expectations. Additionally, future guidance exceeded analysts' estimates, driven by optimism around the growth of the company's artificial intelligence (AI) software.

An overweight position in Astera Labs, Inc. contributed to performance this quarter. The company reported strong third-quarter earnings, including its fifth consecutive record quarterly revenue.

Not owning Nu Holdings Ltd. (NU) contributed to performance this month. The stock declined as concerns grew over Brazil's weakening currency and rising inflation, which could pressure the company's operations.

Additionally, Nu Holdings recently undertook a significant investment in a South African digital bank, raising investor concerns about potential overextension.

Key detractors for the quarter were Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR), Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) and Builders FirstSource, Inc (BLDR).

An overweight position in Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. (MPWR) detracted from performance. The stock declined following a report which suggested that the company's power management chips would not meet the requirements for use in NVIDIA chips.

An underweight position in Coinbase Global, Inc. (COIN) detracted from performance this quarter. The stock appreciated in November following the election of Donald Trump, as cryptocurrency values increased. The rise in crypto prices boosted Coinbase's performance, given its role as a leading platform for trading and exchanging digital currencies.

An overweight position in Builders FirstSource, Inc. (BLDR) detracted from performance. The company's stock was negatively impacted by challenging market conditions in the housing sector, driven primarily by rising bond yields, which have increased borrowing costs and dampened demand in the market.

Current strategy and outlook

After months of noise surrounding the U.S. presidential election, markets have now refocused on macroeconomic data, which offer mixed signals. Key concerns include global geopolitical tensions, especially around tariffs and deteriorating sentiment tied to mega-cap positioning and broader market weakness. Despite these challenges, U.S. equities should continue to benefit from robust consumer spending, optimism around AI and solid corporate earnings. U.S. inflation is projected at 2.5% for 2025, but the new administration's policies could reverse the disinflation trend.

Holdings detail

Companies mentioned in this report percentage of portfolio investments, as of 12/31/24: AppLovin Corp. 4.06%, Astera Labs, Inc. 1.11%, Nu Holdings Ltd. 0.00%, Monolithic Power Systems, Inc. 1.98%, Coinbase Global, Inc. 0.62% and Builders FirstSource, Inc. 1.09%; 0% indicates that the security is no longer in the portfolio. Portfolio holdings are subject to daily change.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Murphy USA: Good Fundamentals Reflected In Valuation (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Murphy USA (NYSE:MUSA) have been a solid performer over the past year, adding 11%, as the owner of gas stations and convenience stores continues to churn out solid results. While shares have pulled back from their late 2024 highs like most stocks, MUSA has held in fairly over the volatile past week, given its business is fairly isolated from tariff pressures. I last covered shares in May 2024, rating MUSA a buy, and since then, the stock has added a strong 14% while the market has shed 4%, making now a good time to revisit MUSA.

Murphy USA is a leading owner of gas stations and associated convenience stores. In total, the company operates over 1,700 locations in 27 states, primarily in the Sun Belt and Midwest. Over the long-term, it has been a steady generator of free cash flow, and it has aggressively returned capital to investors while also growing organically. Since 2013, it has allocated 47% of cash flow to buybacks, cutting its share count by 57%. At the same time, thanks to its investments in modernizing its stores, its loyalty programs, and expansion efforts, its volume growth has considerably outperformed the market.

Ultimately, gasoline is a very low growth market, but it also has limited cyclicality, which is why shares have held up fairly well during the recent spike in volatility. Outside of COVID and its unique economic shutdown, gasoline demand, while clearly exhibiting seasonal patterns, has had fairly modest economic sensitivity. That said, fuel demand is lower than a decade ago.

Now, I do view gasoline as a market in secular decline, though it is a very slowly melting ice cube, rather than a sharp collapse. There, of course, is the growth of electric vehicles (EV), which reduces gasoline consumption. That said, EV penetration growth has slowed. Still, thanks to advances in engine technology, new cars are simply more fuel efficient than ten years ago. All else equal, it is likely that the gasoline-consuming cars built in 5 and 10 years from now will also be somewhat more fuel efficient. As the US auto fleet rolls over and new cars replace older vehicles on the road, we will see ongoing gradual fuel consumption declines.

Indeed in the company’s fourth quarter, Murphy USA reported that fuel volumes were flat at 4.8 billion gallons in 2024 but down 1.1% on a same-store basis. While it can likely keep aggregate sales flattish via new store growth, I expect same-store sales to decline 0.5-1.25% annually over the long run.

Now, in the near-term, I am slightly more optimistic. President Trump has implemented a 25% tariff on imported cars, which will likely make new cars more expensive and reduce the pace of sales all else equal. This will slow the transformation of the fleet and reduce the headwind from improved fuel efficiency. More meaningfully, tariff uncertainty has caused oil and gasoline prices to plunge. As you can see below, gasoline prices have fallen by 27% from a year ago.

This decline is well-timed ahead of the critical summer driving season and may lead to some more driving-based vacations over the summer. To the extent tariffs are inflationary, they will impact goods prices much more than services prices—the cost of a night at a hotel room will be relatively unimpacted. As such, I can envision consumers shifting their discretionary budgets away from goods and towards services, which should be a net positive for domestic gasoline demand.

Additionally, sudden falls in wholesale prices tend to be good for MUSA’s margins. In general, prices at the pump move a bit more slowly than at the wholesale level, so quick plunges cause temporary margin gains while spikes tend to compress margins. Over time, prices at the pump converge, but this will be a favorable tailwind for Q2 results. In 2024, its margin on each gallon of fuel compressed to 30.5cpg (cents per gallon) from 31.4 in 2023. In Q4, it was stable at 32.5cpg aided by the sharp decline you can see above in September. I expect this decline in gasoline prices, should it persist, to lift results in the May-July months.

While known for its gas stations, its convenience stores are the key source of profits for the company. Of course, stopping for gas is the impetus for most customers to walk into the convenience store, so the two businesses are inextricably linked, but much of the profit comes from getting the consumer out of the car and into the store, which is why MUSA has invested in improving the cleanliness and quality of its layouts.

We are seeing results come through. In its most recent quarter, merchandise dollars rose by 5.6% to $209 million with margins expanding 50bps to 19.9%. Nicotine accounts for about 45% of merchandise sales. Both nicotine and non-nicotine were up about 3% on a same-store basis. Importantly, most convenience store products have limited tariff exposure, given the US is a net agricultural exporter. Additionally, like gasoline, c-store sales have proven to be largely recession-proof, even rising during the financial crisis.

I do not view the current economic uncertainty as likely to weigh on the company’s 2025 results. In 2025, MUSA is expecting $1-1.12 billion of EBITDA, up about 6% from 2024 levels at the midpoint. Given some gasoline margin expansion and store growth, I view this as a credible expectation. Last year, MUSA did 79 new stores, and it expects a similar total this year, though with a shift towards new stores, we should see a higher impact on sales. Even with a similar store count, it expected cap-ex to be down slightly.

This is one area where I do see risk. Given tariffs, construction inflation could be worse than we anticipated a few months ago. MUSA could respond to this pressure by slowing its pace of construction, but I expect cap-ex to come in at the high-end of its range. MUSA generated $390 million of 2024 free cash flow. I expect at least $400 million in 2025, given wider gasoline margins but some upside risk to cap-ex. Working capital may also be a modest headwind.

Importantly, Murphy is well positioned to return that cash flow to investors. It has an excellent balance sheet. With $1.8 billion of long-term debt, it has only 1.8x leverage. Given the stability of the business, MUSA could easily manage its business at 2.5x. I do not expect it to lever up, but given its strong balance sheet, we can expect all free cash flow to be focused on capital returns. In February, Murphy did increase its dividend by 2%, but that still leaves shares with just a 0.4% dividend yield. Instead, MUSA prioritizes buybacks, reducing its share count by nearly 5% in 2024. I expect at least 4% share count reduction in over the next twelve months.

When I last covered MUSA, I had targeted $450, with an upside case of $490, which shares briefly surpassed. After their recent pullback, shares sit at my prior target. With about $20-22 of go-forward free cash flow, MUSA has a 4.5%-5% free cash yield. I view that as appropriate. Ultimately, its core business has stable long-term prospects with modest C-store growth offsetting gasoline declines, and it grows its store count ~3% per year for about an 8% long-term return.

After significant outperformance, MUSA appears to be fairly reflecting its fundamentals. As such, I see shares as a “hold.” Now, if we do see the market fall further, I would expect MUSA to fall less and outperform, given its limited tariff exposure. Similarly, on a rebound, it would likely lag. Overall, I see MUSA as a “hold” and a stock that investors can “hide” in to wait out tariff volatility, but I no longer see shares positioned for ongoing long-term outperformance and would want a pullback closer to $400-420 before buying.
